title: Fractions
objective: Students will explore fractions concepts including Fractions of a whole, Equivalent fractions on a number line, Equivalent fractions (age 8+) and more.

steps.content: Let's discover the amazing world of Fractions of a whole!
Understand a fraction 1/b as the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts; understand a/b as a parts of size 1/b
Let's practice together! Given a shape divided into 5 equal parts, identify one shaded part as 1/5
If you cut a pizza into 6 equal slices, can you explain what 1/6 means — and then work out what 4/6 of the pizza looks like?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Fractions of a whole: Given a shape divided into 5 equal parts, identify one shaded part as 1/5 Explain that 3/4 means 3 parts each of size 1/4

lessons.title: Equivalent fractions on a number line

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Equivalent fractions on a number line? Let's find out together!
Understand two fractions as equivalent if they are the same size or the same point on a number line; recognise and show families of common equivalent fractions using diagrams
Time to try this out: Use fraction strips to show 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6
If you sees that 2/4 and 1/2 land on the exact same point on a number line, can they explain in their own words why those two fractions are equal?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Equivalent fractions on a number line: Use fraction strips to show 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 Verify on a number line that 2/3 and 4/6 land on the same point
Have you ever thought about Equivalent fractions (age 8+)? Let's find out together!
Generate simple equivalent fractions and explain why they are equivalent using visual fraction models
Let me show you something cool. Given 1/3, generate 2/6 as equivalent and show with area model
If you is shown a fraction bar split into 3 parts with 1 shaded (= 1/3), can they draw an equivalent bar with 6 parts that shows exactly the same amount shaded?
Great job! Today we learned about Equivalent fractions (age 8+). Given 1/3, generate 2/6 as equivalent and show with area model Simplify 4/8 to 1/2 and justify with a fraction strip
Let's explore Fractions on a number line (age 8+)! Have you ever wondered about this?
Represent fractions on a number line: partition the interval 0 to 1 into b equal parts to locate 1/b, then mark off a lengths of 1/b from 0 to locate a/b
Let's practice together! Partition a 0-to-1 number line into 4 equal parts and mark 1/4
If you draws a number line from 0 to 1 split into 5 equal parts, can they label where 2/5 and 4/5 go — and explain how they worked it out?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Fractions on a number line (age 8+). Partition a 0-to-1 number line into 4 equal parts and mark 1/4 Explain that each part on the line has size 1/b

lessons.title: Decimal equivalents of tenths and hundredths

steps.content: Let's explore Decimal equivalents of tenths and hundredths! Have you ever wondered about this?
Recognise and write decimal equivalents of any number of tenths or hundredths (e.g. 3/10 = 0.3, 27/100 = 0.27)
Let me show you something cool. Write 7/10 as 0.7 and vice versa
If you sees 7/10 on a price label, can they rewrite it as a decimal — and do the same thing for 43/100?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Decimal equivalents of tenths and hundredths. Write 7/10 as 0.7 and vice versa Convert 45/100 to 0.45
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Tenths (age 8+)!
Count up and down in hundredths; recognise that hundredths arise when dividing an object by 100 or dividing tenths by 10
Time to try this out: Count from 3/100 to 12/100 in hundredths
If you has £1 and spends 37p, can they say that 37p is 37/100 of a pound — and count up in hundredths from 0/100 to 100/100?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Tenths (age 8+). Count from 3/100 to 12/100 in hundredths Explain that 1/10 ÷ 10 = 1/100
Let's explore Decimal & Percent Notation! Have you ever wondered about this?

Read, write, and use decimal and percentage notation correctly — decimal, decimal point, tenths, hundredths, thousandths, percentage, per cent, % symbol, convert, terminating decimal — and understand the relationships between fractions, decimals, and percentages as three ways of expressing the same value
Let's practice together! Read and write decimal numbers correctly, identifying the value of each digit (ones, tenths, hundredths)
If you saw '0.5' and '50%' written down, could they explain that they both mean the same amount — and write them as a fraction too?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Decimal & Percent Notation: Read and write decimal numbers correctly, identifying the value of each digit (ones, tenths, hundredths) Use the % symbol correctly and explain that per cent means 'out of 100'
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Fraction-Decimal Equivalents!
Recognise and write decimal equivalents of 1/4, 1/2, and 3/4
Let's practice together! State that 1/2 = 0.5, 1/4 = 0.25, 3/4 = 0.75
If a recipe calls for 3/4 of a cup of flour, can you tell you what that is as a decimal — without looking it up?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Fraction-Decimal Equivalents. State that 1/2 = 0.5, 1/4 = 0.25, 3/4 = 0.75 Match fractions to decimals in a sorting activity
Have you ever thought about Decimal place value (age 8+)? Let's find out together!
Compare numbers with the same number of decimal places up to two decimal places
Time to try this out: Order 0.45, 0.54, 0.39 from smallest to largest
If you is comparing two prices — £4.75 and £4.57 — can they tell you which is more expensive without using a calculator?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Decimal place value (age 8+). Order 0.45, 0.54, 0.39 from smallest to largest Compare 3.72 and 3.27 using place-value reasoning
Today we're going to learn about Dividing by 10 and 100. Get ready for an adventure!
Find the effect of dividing a one- or two-digit number by 10 and 100, identifying the value of the digits as ones, tenths, and hundredths
Let me show you something cool. Calculate 37 ÷ 10 = 3.7 and identify 3 as ones and 7 as tenths
If you starts with the number 48 and divides it by 10, can they tell you the answer — and explain what happened to each digit's place value?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Dividing by 10 and 100. Calculate 37 ÷ 10 = 3.7 and identify 3 as ones and 7 as tenths Calculate 4 ÷ 100 = 0.04 and identify 4 as hundredths
Today we're going to learn about Adding Fractions (Same Denominator). Get ready for an adventure!
Add and subtract fractions with the same denominator, including results greater than one whole (e.g. 5/8 + 6/8 = 11/8)
Let's practice together! Calculate 3/5 + 4/5 = 7/5 and explain it equals 1 2/5
If a recipe uses 5/8 of a bag of flour and then another 6/8, can you work out the total and explain what the answer means as a mixed number?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Adding Fractions (Same Denominator): Calculate 3/5 + 4/5 = 7/5 and explain it equals 1 2/5 Subtract 2/6 from 5/6
Have you ever thought about Comparing fractions (age 8+)? Let's find out together!
Compare two fractions with the same numerator or the same denominator by reasoning about size; record comparisons with >, =, or < symbols
Time to try this out: Compare 3/8 and 3/4: same numerator, larger denominator means smaller pieces so 3/8 < 3/4
If you sees 3/5 and 3/8, can they tell you which is bigger just by thinking about the size of each piece — without needing to draw it out?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Comparing fractions (age 8+). Compare 3/8 and 3/4: same numerator, larger denominator means smaller pieces so 3/8 < 3/4 Compare 5/6 and 2/6: same denominator so 5/6 > 2/6
Let's discover the amazing world of Fractions of a whole (age 8+)!
Express whole numbers as fractions (e.g. 3 = 3/1) and recognise fractions equivalent to whole numbers (e.g. 4/4 = 1, 6/1 = 6)
Time to try this out: Write 5 as 5/1 and explain why
If you ask you to write the number 3 as a fraction, can they write 3/1 — and also tell you what fraction equals exactly 1 whole, like 4/4 or 5/5?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Fractions of a whole (age 8+): Write 5 as 5/1 and explain why Locate 4/4 and 1 at the same point on a number line
Let's discover the amazing world of Fractions of amounts (harder)!
Solve problems involving increasingly harder fractions to calculate quantities, including non-unit fractions where the answer is a whole number
Let me show you something cool. Find 3/5 of 20
If 3/4 of a class of 28 pupils brought their homework in, can you work out exactly how many pupils that is?
Great job! Today we learned about Fractions of amounts (harder). Find 3/5 of 20 Calculate 2/3 of 18 and explain the two-step process (divide then multiply)
Let's discover the amazing world of Decimals and fractions!
Solve simple measure and money problems involving fractions and decimals to two decimal places
Time to try this out: Calculate 1/4 of £3.20
If a bottle holds 1.5 litres and you drinks 0.75 litres, can they work out how much is left — and write it as both a decimal and a fraction?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Decimals and fractions: Calculate 1/4 of £3.20 A rope is 2.5 m long; how much is left after cutting 0.75 m?
Let's explore Decimal place value! Have you ever wondered about this?
Round decimals with one decimal place to the nearest whole number
Time to try this out: Round 3.7 to 4 and 3.2 to 3
If you measures something as 3.7 m, can they tell you whether that rounds to 3 m or 4 m — and explain why?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Decimal place value: Round 3.7 to 4 and 3.2 to 3 Place 6.5 on a number line between 6 and 7 and decide it rounds to 7
Let's explore Fractions as parts of shapes! Have you ever wondered about this?
Partition shapes into parts with equal areas and express the area of each part as a unit fraction of the whole
Let's practice together! Partition a rectangle into 6 equal-area parts and label each 1/6
If you ask you to fold a square piece of paper into 8 equal parts, can they tell you what fraction of the whole square each part represents?
Great job! Today we learned about Fractions as parts of shapes. Partition a rectangle into 6 equal-area parts and label each 1/6 Show two different ways to partition a square into 4 equal parts
